sentences.text: Nathan Aptekar, A-P-T-E-K-A-R.
Zdzilowice, a small town in Poland.
Z-D-Z-I-L-O-W-I-C-E.
Eljezer.
E-L-J-E-Z-E-R.
Salesman.
Coals and, what do they call it?
Paint, but the paint came in a type of stones.
You had to put them in the water to create paint.
That was not so easy, like today, you go to the store and buy already paint.
In the city, Lodz, L-O-D-Z.
When I was four years old.
It was the biggest industrial city in Poland when it comes to clothing.
Yeah, we used to go with my father to the synagogues.
Large, yes.
Very large.
In 87 Street [POLISH].
In Polish they call [POLISH].
Yes.
It was a typical cluster of homes, five, six, story building, with a big yard in the middle.
All the windows were facing to the yard.
Many times peddlers used to come in, musicians, to throw down from the windows some charity for them.
Playing in the yard, going to school.
Mostly Poland was soccer, was one of the famous games.
Mostly, yes.
They could not play with anybody else.
Almost every day on the way to school.
If I was lucky, I was not beaten up.
My books were ripped out many times, the homework disappeared.
Until I got older, and I ask all the kids to go with me together, not to go alone.
Actually, if one thinks today, it's difficult to understand, when you go to school you have to be afraid to be beaten up from your neighbor kids.
And was calling names, like in English it would come out, dirty Jew, in Polish, [POLISH].
And this was a daily, what they call your name, [NON-ENGLISH].
That means, Jews go to Israel, Palestine.
Awful, depressed.
When you came to school, you actually could not study after going through this type of experience.
Yes.
I had to do two years to finish the first grade.
Chaja, C-H-A-J-A.
She was working in a clothing factory.
And the rest, doing the cooking, taking care of the kids.
Yes.
After coming back from Daven with my father, always the candles were lit.
We all make kiddish, and normal food and [NON-ENGLISH] to sing at the meal, and after the meal.
It was strictly, strictly religious, a kosher home.
One brother, and two sisters.
My brother's name is Icek, older sister's name is Regina, and the middle one, Ester.
Regina, she is now 75.
Ester, is probably about five years younger.
And my brother, Icek, is four years older from me.
Yes.
Knitting, whenever she had time, she liked to sit and knit, making sweaters, scarves, gloves.
Shawls in Poland is called [POLISH].
I could say, yes.
Whatever time we had, we were all knitting together.
And the rest, they were going to school.
Same time like I was going, but they went to different schools.
Yes, before even I went to the public school, I was having a [NON-ENGLISH] they call it in Jewish, a teacher that was teaching me Hebrew.
Davening which I knew very well at that time.
Nine years.
1939 When the first sirens started and the bombs were flying.
And I remember like today when I looked out from the hiding places we were on, we were seeing the-- some Polish people, whatever, worked together with the Germans.
I don't remember the name on them, but they were staying on the roofs, signaling to the planes where to bomb.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And Lodz was very famous for it.
So the German has a very easy job knowing exactly where to hit.
And after two weeks they just came into the city.
Yes.
We were hiding can in the basements of the big buildings.
But it wasn't big bombings over there, just they were bombing the electric station, which was right in the corner from us, the post office, government buildings mostly.
Of course.
Could a nine-year-old guy not be afraid?
After the bombings started, the Germans came into the city.
And the first order of the day was that all the Jews have to wear yellow belts on the arm.
Honestly, at that time, as a young kid, I figured it's something like a treat.
I put it on and walked out in the street, the first one.
Till my mother pulled me back not to go.
I figured it's something cute to wear, the band.
I didn't understand.
But then, passed by, maybe a week or 10 days, was when all of the Jews to concentrate in one place to the ghetto, that they made.
I think Lodz was the first Jewish ghetto created.
No.
We, in the nighttime, escaped from the city through walking out.
It took us several days to reach the town, Zdzilowice, where my mother was born over there.
And the grandparents were still living.
Yes.
This was in the end of 1939.
I remember it was very cold.
Difficult to remember dates.
But I think it was around October.
We walked several days.
And then we with farmers, when they was going to the bazaars like here.
How do you call bazaar?
Flea markets.
And we had the rides with them slowly, till we arrived to the town.
We moved in to the grandparents.
And we were living over there.
That was very difficult from dislocating from the place, from a big city, without any job, without mean of getting jobs.
But somehow the parents managed.
Me and my brother was going to the woods, summertime, to collect leftover branches from trees, to having for cooking and heating for the house.
The grandmother was name was Ita.
And the grandparent name was Moshe.
Mother's.
Aptekar.
Klinkofman.
K-L-I-N-K-O-F-M-A-N
No.
None of the Jewish were allowed to go to any school after the Germans stopped there.
So by that time, all the education was finished.
Just going to the woods to collect the wood, which I mentioned before, almost every day summertime, to have it for the winter.
Until October, November 1942, when all the war, said all the Jews have to concentrate in big cities to be relocated.
That was the normal, telling us that we get relocated to places where there's jobs and other thing.
I do remember there was discussions when I went with my father to the synagogue.
Nobody actually knew exactly what happened.
Nobody like what's happening.
Everybody was looking for places to wherever it's still not Judenrein, but that people can still live.
And my parents picked a city not far from Lublin.
The city's name is Turobin, T-O-R-O-B-I-N.
We went over there.
We had relatives.
And we were not the only one in the city.
It was so crowded that every family had maybe 10 families living with them, from relatives, from all over towns, they were coming over there.
We were still allowed to live normal.
This was by the end of 1942 when they made the Judenrein.
I think it's the end.
It's difficult to remember exactly the dates, but each area was different dates.
But in this city, Turobin, we could not stay too long.
From there was the order that all has to go to Majdanek, to concentrate.
And I remembered the last day we went to the shul, synagogue, to pray, to say Tehillim.
Tehillim is a special prayer in the Bible.
And for every misfortune, funerals, you say Tehillim special prayers.
And we were praying that maybe God will help us and the German would change their mind not to go the Majdanek.
We knew already it's something not correct.
I remember like today, that the rabbi had a speech by the end of the Tehillim praying.
And he said in Hebrew, [HEBREW].
This is, in English, [HEBREW] would be friends, or it came the time to go to be sacrificed.
I could not agree with him at all, being 9 years old, or 10 years.
This was something.
At that time, I was10 years old.
I could not understand how to go to be sacrificed.
Why?
So on the way home, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember like today, my father was crying.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SOBBING] And when we come back, he said to mother, take the kids and go away.
You will survive.
In the morning, we got dressed like farmers.
We took shovels.
There are special shovels where they dig potatoes.
It's like the end of the stick, the shovel is like this for digging potatoes.
We took on the arms.
And we went out of the city.
It was the only bridge the German wasn't watching.
But they did not recognize.
My father did not want to take off the beard.
He was very religious.
We, the boys, me and my brother, cut off the [HEBREW].
And we put up the vest is like the farmers.
And we left.
And we came back to the town, Zdzilowice.
We went into a Polish family, a gentile family, by the name, Matisek, M-A-T-I-S-E-K.
They kept us in the attic from the house, that's [INAUDIBLE] there.
After we were there upstairs in this attic about three weeks.
In this three weeks, they dug out a bunker behind the kitchen, going to the bedroom.
That was a two room house.
In the corner of the kitchen was a fireplace like here.
And they kept the wood, where the wood is, they made the entrance to the bunker.
And this was a dug bunker.
We were there for six months.
He went with the rabbi, with all his friends, to Majdanek.
And that was the end of it.
In the bunker we were sitting all the time inside.
But this was tremendous, how to say it?
It was very difficult to leave and not to get killed from Polish bandits, whatever, just chasing to get Jews.
They get 5 kilo sugar for it.
So the family, whatever food they had, this was a poor family, Matisek.
We were with them.
They only give us a couple of boiled potatoes a day.
And we survived on this one.
After the six months, we started going out in the night to get some food.
And mostly, we went out, my older sister, Regina, and me, as the youngest one.
We went out early in the morning, 4 o'clock, and came back very late in the night, nobody to follow us where we're going.
Yes.
We went to some gentile families.
They were very kind enough.
Even they would endanger themselves, they were giving us food.
It was the first the firemen in the city, in the town, they called them in Polish, the [POLISH].
But this was the volunteer fire department.
They were catching.
For each Jew what they caught, they got 5 kilo sugar.
The Germans.
And there were other ones, didn't care for the sugar.
They were just catching and killing.
As a matter of fact, this family, Matisek, where we were hiding in the bunker, their grandson was one of the biggest killers in town.
Every couple night, he came in the evening sitting, and talking, and explaining how many he killed, and how he killed, with a hatchet, with a shovel.
And we were hearing everything.
Yes.
But we know everybody.
We found out from the family where we were.
But then, who is the killers and who is not?
And we know the people who used to live over there.
So we know who to go.
And by the end of the six months being the bunker, the fact that we had to leave this place-- let me retract that.
One night, my sister, Regina, left in the morning for food.
And that time was really quiet in town, so Mrs.
Matisek ask us that we can already sit a little bit in the kitchen the night to straighten out.
There was no light, nothing.
And about 10, 11 o'clock in the night, we hear a big noise.
Four of, I think it was four of them, from the bandits, they caught my sister, Regina.
And they were beating her.
They followed her, actually, they burst into the house together with her.
And they find mother, sister, my brother, sitting in the kitchen.
I, at that time, jumped under the bed, slide over.
They did not see me.
They were beating them with the other end of the rifle, that the blood was all over the wall.
Actually, they left them.
They were laying without movement.
And she was begging them, Mrs.
Matisek, to them in Polish.
She sweared them by God, leave them alone.
They already have death, like this.
And finally they left.
So when they left, I crawled out from under the bed.
And we took some water.
We washed them, Mrs.
Matisek and me, until they come to themself.
We went on the attic, not in the house already, just on the barn, where they had to keep the cows.
We were there maybe a week.
But we were afraid to already to be over there, should they come back and finish off.
But they were in no position to move.
In that time we changed.
We went to another family by the name of Golec.
When we went into them, it was by the end of 1942.
It was around April, May, of 1943.
On the top of where the cows are in the barn, upstairs, until it took three, four, weeks to build a bunker under where the cows stayed.
And many times, when the cows were peeing, it was straight going down to us.
So we had not only sit dry, we were sitting in wet.
And we were there almost five, close to six months.
Mostly two, three, potatoes daily, and some bread and water.
We had a small opening to the outside, maybe 2 inches, for fresh air.
And this was the light and the fresh air.
No.
We were afraid, after the beating already, to leave until after that time.
But by the end of 1943, I started already to go out in town to people what I knew.
And several of them was putting me food under a stone.
A family, Pison, they used to be our neighbors.
And he was afraid.
He didn't want his kids to know that he is helping me.
So he put food under a stone.
And he said, Nathan, if the food will be there, I know that you're not alive.
If the food is not there, I'll put other food.
And this was going on for months like this.
Pison, P-I-S-O-N.
I visited the family last year, but only the kids.
And the older daughter, when she used to play with me, when we were nine, 10 old, she said, I knew my father was helping you.
Needless to say, we were very well.
And when we came to them, they didn't know what to do for us.
This was now a year ago, after the war.
But going back to that time when I was going out in the town already, we were hearing one evening shots.
And we found out the next day that my mother's sister, and her husband, and two of their boys were killed by the people who hide them, not far from us, maybe six houses away.
They took them out on the field.
Everybody was hearing the way-- my uncle's name was Chaim.
I remember like today.
He was begging them, calling by the name of the people.
I will get you whatever I can get together.
I have hidden gold here, and there.
Just don't kill me.
Nothing helped.
They shot and buried them in the place.
Yes.
No, I don't remember the name now, but I can find out the name.
But the question was that time, that we know that they will not to let any witnesses, any relatives to survive after the war.
So I decided to go to them for food.
And they were very, very polite, invite me to eat breakfast with them together.
And they were asking where my family is.
I said, we all separate.
One doesn't know from the other, in case he gets caught.
But if one will get caught, the families will burn them down.
And I continue coming to them for months for food.
Especially for them to think that we don't know that they did it.
So they did not look for us.
I was at that time already 13 years.
Never had.
No.
The only thing what a person in that position was thinking, is how to survive, not to be caught.
And the biggest wish was to go to sleep and not to wake up.
About every day, you're finding out who else was killed from the people that they were hiding.
And the numbers was coming down drastically.
And we know all these people.
And the question was, who was next?
And there were everyday, was a next person.
And they wasn't killed like a rifle.
They were mostly killed like they're doing in Mexico with hatchets, or the other.
In our area that's what it was.
Nothing.
Trying to survive.
Not to let yourself to be caught.
Going out in the morning, one day I collected some bread from several people.
It was too early to go back to our place.
So I went under a barn, where there were some blocks, staying under the floor, and fell asleep.
When I woke up, I didn't have the bread.
I'm looking outside to see a duck is holding the bag, the sack.
And he's still eating.
So I jumped on him.
And it's left over, one piece.
So the duck walked away.
He wasn't hungry.
So he didn't bite me.
And I came back with one piece of bread.
Until late in the night.
Then I returned to the place.
Yes.
By the end of 1943, these people didn't want to hold us any longer.
They were afraid.
And we went to another Polish family, but they did not have any food to give us.
So the only food what we can organize is to go around like I did.
One day, me and my brother went.
And we figured, we'll bring food, whatever we can.
And we took ourselves green peas, what they were keeping under a house, to dry.
So we took sheets.
We put the green peas inside.
Yeah, green peas it was.
And we carried on the shoulder like inexperienced thieves.
And probably we were leaving signs.
Until the next day in the morning, we hear something, a noise outside.
We were in the attic.
We're looking down.
Guys with such a long sticks, and said, we'll have to kill these thieves.
They didn't know who did it.
And they came actually into the barn.
And I was that time, me, my brother-- we both brought the green peas-- and mother.
The split decision was to rip off the roof.
It was a straw roof.
Me and my brother jumped down.
And we run away.
The caught my mother.
They didn't do nothing to mother, but they knew that we escaped.
